reviewerResults.persona: Small startup CTO with low to medium AWS experience. Cares most about time-to-production and shipping quickly without getting trapped in AWS complexity.
strengths: Clean, scannable structure that mirrors the reviewed example page — short H2 labels, focused CodeBlock examples, and a practical 'when to use' decision table with concrete alternatives.
Pricing context is upfront and grounded in the source JSDoc (~$1/million requests, no base cost). The zero-cost-at-idle point is exactly what a startup CTO weighing Lambda vs. containers cares about.

The 29-second integration timeout warning is surfaced twice (routing section + Warning block) — this is the single most common gotcha for teams discovering API Gateway, and it's appropriately prominent.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with proper focus markers. Application code (Hono handler) uses a fenced block. No forbidden patterns.

The basic example demonstrates the most common real-world pattern (catch-all proxy route with framework-level routing via Hono) and includes a working handler — a startup CTO can copy both files and deploy.
Routing section is well-structured: three path formats explained concisely, multi-function example shows a realistic split (users + orders), and specificity ordering is mentioned.
problems: Authorizers are mentioned in a single sentence ('Each integration can specify an authorizer to protect the route') with no example or explanation. For a startup CTO, protecting API routes is a day-one concern — this is the biggest content gap on the page.

The container workload Info block claims 'only payload format 1.0 is supported' and 'All integrations on a container workload must target the same container port.' Neither constraint is visible in the provided source types (ContainerWorkloadHttpApiIntegrationProps). These may be runtime validations, but they are not grounded in the provided source files.

The name-reference pattern (httpApiGatewayName: 'gateway' referencing the key in the resources object) is never explicitly explained. A reader unfamiliar with Stacktape's resource-name convention will wonder how 'gateway' the string connects to 'gateway' the variable.

'Route53 hosted zone in your AWS account' appears in both the custom domains and basic example sections without a brief explanation or link. A startup CTO with low AWS experience may not know what a hosted zone is or how to create one.

The alarms section lists two alarm types and mentions global alarms but provides no example configuration. Every other feature section on the page includes a CodeBlock — the inconsistency makes alarms feel like an afterthought.
mandatoryFixes: Add a brief authorizer subsection or example under the Routing section. At minimum, show a JWT authorizer example using the HttpApiIntegration's `authorizer` property, since auth is critical for the target audience. A 5-line focused CodeBlock showing an authorizer on one route would suffice.

Either ground the container workload constraints ('only payload format 1.0', 'same container port') in a specific source reference, or soften the language to 'check the container workload docs for integration constraints' with a link. Do not state constraints that cannot be verified from the provided source.

Add a one-sentence explanation of the name-reference pattern near the basic example — e.g., 'The httpApiGatewayName must match the key you use for the gateway in the resources object.' This is a Stacktape-specific convention the reader needs to understand.
optionalImprovements: Add a brief parenthetical or link for 'Route53 hosted zone' — e.g., 'Your domain must have a Route53 hosted zone (a DNS zone managed in your AWS account) ...' or link to a setup guide. This helps the low-AWS-experience reader.
Add a small CodeBlock example in the alarms section showing a latency alarm configuration. This would make the section consistent with the rest of the page and more actionable.

Consider adding a brief note about the AWS Free Tier for API Gateway (first 1 million requests/month free for 12 months) since this is mentioned in the source type JSDoc for the web-service comparison and is relevant for cost-conscious startups.

The payload format section could benefit from a one-line recommendation: 'Use 2.0 for new projects unless you depend on multiValueHeaders.' The current text says 2.0 is 'recommended for new projects' but buries the practical implication of 1.0 (multiValueHeaders/multiValueQueryStringParameters) in the same sentence as the default.
In the 'when to use' table, the 'Web application firewall (WAF) at API level' row could link to the web-app-firewall resource page for completeness.

reviewerResults.persona: SMB engineering manager with medium to high AWS knowledge. Cares about long-term maintainability, team clarity, operational fit, technically correct Stacktape class-based config examples, and valid MDX component usage.
strengths: Uses the required TypeScript class-based config pattern with <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/> and no hand-authored Stacktape YAML.
Strong early decision framing with clear “use this / use something else” guidance.
Basic example is realistic and copyable, and the application handler is correctly shown as ordinary fenced TypeScript.
API reference and ReferenceableParams components are present with the correct resource-oriented shape.
problems: Several technical claims are not grounded in the provided source files, especially the 29-second API Gateway timeout, 504 behavior, route uniqueness, route matching by specificity, and WebSocket/WAF alternative guidance.

The page says HTTP API Gateway “scales to zero when idle” and “No base cost — you pay nothing when there's no traffic”; the source supports pay-per-request pricing, but does not explicitly state scale-to-zero or no base-cost behavior.
The first paragraph says “Built on AWS API Gateway V2”; the provided source names HTTP API Gateway and AWS API Gateway concepts but does not ground the V2 implementation detail.

The CORS section lists specific “common headers” (`Content-Type`, `Authorization`, `X-Api-Key`, and others) that are not enumerated in the provided source; only permissive defaults and common headers are sourced.
The CDN section claims CloudFront pricing has no monthly base cost and charges per request and GB transferred, but no concrete CDN pricing source is provided for this page.
The access logging section says logs are viewable in the Stacktape Console, but the source only grounds `stacktape logs` for this resource.

The alarms section mentions global alarms from the Stacktape Console. The source supports merging with global alarms from the Console, but the draft also says “or use global alarms from the Stacktape Console,” which needs careful wording because the provided source only documents merge/exclusion behavior, not the Console workflow.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or narrow unsupported 29-second timeout claims unless a provided source file grounds the API Gateway integration timeout and resulting 504 behavior.
Remove or narrow unsupported claims about route uniqueness and route specificity unless runtime validators or generated schema sources are provided to ground them.
Remove unsupported pricing claims beyond `~$1/million requests` unless a provided pricing source grounds no base cost and CDN request/GB pricing.
Remove or source the “AWS API Gateway V2” implementation detail.
Narrow the CORS defaults wording to what the source says: permissive defaults (`*` origins, common headers) and auto-detected methods; do not list specific default headers unless sourced.
optionalImprovements: Add a short note that `payloadFormat: '2.0'` is recommended for new projects, while preserving the sourced default of `1.0` for integrations.
Make “Use something else” more source-grounded by avoiding alternatives whose constraints are not present in the provided files, or cite additional source files for those resources.
Add a compact example for `payloadFormat: '2.0'` because it is a key decision for new Lambda APIs and is directly grounded in the source.

In the container integration note, link it to the exact `multi-container-workload` page if that route exists, and keep the limitation wording exactly scoped to the provided `ContainerWorkloadHttpApiIntegrationProps` source.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer with little AWS or DevOps experience. Needs jargon kept under control and wants examples that are easy to follow.
strengths: Strong opening paragraph — self-contained, defines what the resource is, includes pricing, and works as an AI-extractable snippet.
Excellent 'When to use' section with a concrete comparison table that helps the reader decide between alternatives without needing deep AWS knowledge.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> and application code uses fenced blocks — no forbidden patterns.
The Routing section is well-structured with three path format examples and a realistic multi-function config that shows the most common patterns.
The 29-second timeout warning is high-signal practical guidance that saves the reader from a common surprise.
Headings are consistently short and scannable (all H2s are 1-3 words).
The Info box explaining that web services create an HTTP API Gateway automatically is excellent disambiguation for beginners.
Key claims verified against source: payload format defaults, uniqueness constraint, container workload 1.0-only restriction, and same-format-per-Lambda enforcement are all accurate.
problems: The Alarms section is thin — it lists the two alarm types and their trigger names but provides no example config. For a developer new to AWS, an example showing how to set up a basic error-rate alarm would make this actionable rather than just informational.

The Info box about container workloads uses the internal resource type name 'multi-container-workload' in parentheses without linking to a docs page or explaining what it is. A developer with little AWS experience won't know what this refers to.

Authorizer configuration is mentioned in passing ('Each integration can specify an authorizer to protect the route') but there is no example, no link to an authorizers page, and no explanation of what authorizer types are available. Protecting API routes is a core concern for most APIs.

The Payload format section says 'Set payloadFormat on the gateway to apply it globally, or override it per-integration on individual Lambda functions' but doesn't show an example of the per-integration override. Since the section calls out this capability, an example or at least a pointer would help.

The CDN section is almost identical to the web-service example page's CDN section (same sentence structure, same note about additional options). While some repetition across pages is expected, the HTTP API Gateway CDN section could mention that CDN custom domains attach differently than gateway-level custom domains, since this is a common point of confusion.
mandatoryFixes: Add a brief authorizer mention with either a short example or an explicit link to an authorizers/authentication docs page. Protecting routes is too important for the target audience to be a one-sentence aside.

Replace the parenthetical '(multi-container-workload)' in the container workload Info box with a link to the relevant resource page and a short plain-language description (e.g., 'Container workloads can also route through an HTTP API Gateway — see [multi-container workload](/resources/compute/multi-container-workload)').
optionalImprovements: Add a small alarm example in the Alarms section showing a basic error-rate alarm configuration to make the section actionable.
In the Payload format section, add a brief note or example showing how to override payloadFormat at the integration level, since the section explicitly calls out this capability.
In the CDN section, add one sentence noting that custom domains on the CDN use a separate configuration path from gateway-level custom domains, to preempt a common mistake.

Consider adding a brief 'Limits' or 'Constraints' callout box that consolidates the key limits (29s timeout, 10 MB payload, 1000 routes per API) if these are present in the source — this would be a high-value reference for the target audience.
The Access logging section could mention that access logs are separate from Lambda execution logs — both exist and serve different purposes. This distinction trips up beginners.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is factually strong. All major technical claims — pricing, container workload constraints, 29-second timeout, CORS defaults, logging defaults, alarm types, routing patterns, and payload format behavior — are confirmed by source code and runtime validators. Four low-severity issues remain: payload format 1.0/2.0 structural descriptions come from AWS docs not the Stacktape source, CDN pricing and feature list cannot be verified from the provided CdnConfiguration type (not in source set), and 'Built on AWS API Gateway V2' is not stated in source. None of these are incorrect — they are unsupported by the provided evidence or could be tightened.

The draft is broadly aligned with the provided HTTP API Gateway type sources, but it contains several unsupported or over-specific claims. The biggest problems are claims about API Gateway V2, runtime route uniqueness, deployment output behavior, CDN pricing, WAF alternatives, Console logging/global alarms, and container integration constraints that are not grounded in the provided source files.

suggestions: Add an FAQ section (3-5 questions) to capture long-tail queries: 'What is the timeout limit for HTTP API Gateway?', 'HTTP API Gateway vs Application Load Balancer — when to use which?', 'How much does AWS HTTP API Gateway cost?', 'How to configure CORS on HTTP API Gateway in Stacktape?', 'What payload formats does API Gateway V2 support?'

Include AWS API Gateway V2 hard limits — max payload size (10 MB), max routes per API (300), default throttle rate (10,000 req/s burst, 5,000 sustained). These are high-value concrete specifics that AI engines strongly prefer and that users actively search for.

Rename the 'CDN' heading to 'CDN (CloudFront)' — users search for 'CloudFront' by name, and the parenthetical helps both search engines and AI engines associate this section with the right AWS service without hurting readability.

The 'request throttling' capability is promised in the opening paragraph but never elaborated. Add a short section or a paragraph under an existing section explaining how to configure throttle limits (or state that defaults apply and link to AWS docs). This closes a gap between what the answer block promises and what the page delivers.

Rename 'Basic example' to 'Quick start' or 'Minimal setup' — 'basic example' is generic filler that doesn't match any search intent. 'Quick start' aligns with how developers actually search ('HTTP API Gateway quick start').

In the CORS section, make the first paragraph more self-contained by adding 'in Stacktape' or 'on AWS API Gateway V2' — right now 'the gateway level' requires page context. AI engines extract individual paragraphs, so 'Configure CORS on your HTTP API Gateway at the gateway level in Stacktape...' reads better in isolation.

The pricing callout (~$1/million requests) is strong but would be even more discoverable as a structured element (e.g., a table or definition list) with both request pricing and data transfer costs, since 'how much does API Gateway cost' is a top search query.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer with little AWS or DevOps experience. Needs jargon kept under control and wants examples that are easy to follow.
strengths: Strong opening paragraph — self-contained, defines what an HTTP API Gateway is, why it matters, and mentions pricing. Works well as an AI-extractable snippet.
The 'When to use' section is well-structured with a clear table of alternatives. Decisions are opinionated and concrete, helping readers pick the right resource quickly.
The explanation of `httpApiGatewayName` matching the resource key in the `resources` object is a high-value callout that prevents a common beginner mistake.
Routing section is thorough — covers all three path formats (exact, parameter, greedy) with clear explanations and a realistic multi-function example.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with imports hidden via focus markers. Application code uses fenced blocks. No forbidden patterns.
Access logging section covers defaults, supported formats, and log forwarding with grounded pricing (~$0.03/GB from source JSDoc). Good density.
Alarms section shows both available trigger types with a practical example and explains evaluation tuning and notification targets — enough to get started without overwhelming.
problems: The authorizer section describes Cognito as 'validates JWTs issued by a user-auth-pool' but doesn't mention `identitySources` (which controls where the token is read from). For the low-AWS-experience audience, a one-sentence note that it defaults to the Authorization header would prevent confusion when readers try to use it.

The payload format section explains what the two versions are but doesn't give the reader enough to decide between them. It says '2.0 is simpler' (echoing source JSDoc) without showing a concrete difference or saying 'use 2.0 unless you have existing 1.0 handlers'. A beginner won't know what 'simpler event structure' means in practice.

The 'When to use' table lists 'Long-running request processing' pointing to ALB or async pattern, but the source types don't establish a specific timeout limit for HTTP API Gateway integrations. The Lambda 29-second integration timeout (API Gateway limit) is a real constraint but isn't mentioned — this is the actual number the reader needs.

Route53 hosted zone is explained in the custom domains section ('a DNS configuration that tells AWS how to route traffic for your domain') but not in the 'When to use' table's opening paragraph. Since custom domains appear on multiple sections, the explanation should be consistent. The first mention in the intro doesn't explain it at all.

The CDN section is thin — one sentence of explanation, one code example, and a 'see CDN documentation' link. Compared to the example page's CDN section which mentions pricing and supported load balancing types, this section could add one line about CDN having no monthly base cost to help readers decide.
mandatoryFixes: In the payload format section, add a concrete recommendation: tell the reader to use `2.0` for new projects and only use `1.0` for backward compatibility with existing handlers. One sentence showing the practical difference (e.g., 2.0 puts cookies in a parsed array, flattens query strings) would help beginners decide.

In the 'When to use' table row for 'Long-running request processing', add the specific constraint: API Gateway has a 29-second integration timeout for Lambda, so workloads needing longer processing should use an ALB or async pattern. Without the number, the recommendation is vague.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-line note in the authorizer section that Cognito defaults to reading the JWT from the `Authorization` header, so readers know they don't need to configure `identitySources` for the common case.

In the CDN section, add a one-line pricing context (no monthly base cost, pay per request and transfer) to match the density of the example page's CDN section and help readers decide whether to enable it.

Consider adding a brief 'How routing works' mental model before the routing example — something like 'The gateway itself has no routes. Each Lambda function declares which requests it handles via HttpApiIntegration events.' This two-sentence framing exists in the text but is easy to miss before the code block.
The 'Gateway outputs' heading breaks from the convention used in the example page ('Referenceable parameters'). Consider renaming to 'Referenceable parameters' for cross-page consistency.

In the authorizer code example, consider adding a brief comment showing the Lambda authorizer pattern as well (even a 2-line comment), since the Cognito example alone may not help readers using API keys or custom auth.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is exceptionally well-grounded. Every major claim — property names, defaults, types, pricing, alarm triggers, authorizer shapes, log forwarding details, CORS behavior, routing formats, and payload format defaults — was verified against the source type definitions. The only issue found is a minor wording deviation where the source says payload format 2.0 is 'recommended for new projects' but the draft narrows this to 'recommended for new Lambda integrations'. All code examples follow the correct MDX/CodeBlock conventions with no forbidden patterns detected.

The draft is mostly aligned with the provided HTTP API Gateway types, integration types, and alarm types, but it overstates several behaviors that are not grounded in the supplied sources. The main problems are claims about automatic CORS management, HTTP API route matching details, CDN/log-forwarding implementation details, and custom-domain certificate/DNS behavior when DNS record creation is disabled.

suggestions: Shorten heading 'Basic Lambda API example' to 'Basic usage' or 'Quick start' — it's the longest H2 and the surrounding context already makes it clear this is a Lambda example.

Add a brief 'Limits' or 'Quotas' callout near the top or in the 'When to use' section: AWS HTTP API Gateway has a 30-second integration timeout and a 300-route limit per API. These are among the most-searched constraints and help AI engines answer 'what are the limits of HTTP API Gateway' queries.

Add an FAQ section (3-5 entries) at the bottom before the API reference. Natural candidates: 'What is the pricing for HTTP API Gateway?', 'What is the difference between HTTP API Gateway and Application Load Balancer?', 'How do I add a custom domain to HTTP API Gateway?', 'What is the timeout limit for HTTP API Gateway?'. These map directly to high-volume search queries and are easily extractable by AI answer engines.

The payload format section's default-fallback explanation ('The gateway-level property has no default of its own — when neither the gateway nor the integration specifies a format, the integration default of 1.0 applies') is hard to parse in one read. Simplify to: 'Default: 1.0. Set payloadFormat on the gateway to override the default for all integrations. Individual integrations can override the gateway setting.'

In the 'When to use' table, add a row for 'WebSocket or real-time bidirectional communication' pointing to a WebSocket API or alternative — this is a common disambiguation query that AI engines surface when comparing API Gateway types.

The CORS section's permissive-defaults sentence ('* origins, common headers, and methods auto-detected from your integrations') would be stronger if it listed the actual default allowed methods and headers, since 'common headers' is vague and less useful for AI extraction.
